objective: Let τ(n) count the positive divisors of n. Is there some n > 24 such that max over m < n of (m + τ(m)) is at most n + 2?
completion_criteria: A positive answer is one explicit integer plus a bounded exact divisor-count check.
non_results: Finite computation outside its stated range is not a proof of a universal claim.
A candidate is not a solution until independent and human review are recorded.
Restating a known reduction, checking examples, or giving an unproved key lemma is not terminal success.
synthesis_summary: No candidate was found. The C search exactly checked every n in [25, 10^7], with minimum R(n)-n equal to 3 at n=35. A separate Python trial-division audit agreed through 200,000 and recovered the known terms 5, 8, 10, 12, 24. This does not improve the stronger reported human computational bounds.

strategies.title: Exact divisor-incidence sieve with the recurrence R(n+1)=max(R(n), n+tau(n)), independently audited by trial-division factorization and direct maximization.
strategies.mechanism: Exact divisor-incidence sieve with the recurrence R(n+1)=max(R(n), n+tau(n)), independently audited by trial-division factorization and direct maximization.

next_session.objective: Independently review or run the reported frontier certificate through approximately 6.16e17.
